original_abstract:
Self-assembling molecular drugs bridge the gap between the synthetic simplicity of small-molecule chemotherapy and the superior tumor-targeting capabilities of nanoparticle conjugates. However, the successful implementation of this strategy depends on supramolecular interactions strong enough to persist within the complex physiological environment of a living animal. In this study, we demonstrate that metallophilic interactions between cyclometalated palladium complexes facilitate the formation of stable supramolecular nanostructures directly in vivo. Using a skin melanoma mouse model, we found that these self-assembled structures exhibit an extended blood circulation time exceeding 12 hours and achieve a remarkably high tumor accumulation rate of up to 10.2% of the injected dose per gram. 

Upon activation with green light, the palladium assemblies trigger a potent type I photodynamic effect, resulting in efficient tumor destruction. In vitro studies further confirmed this efficacy, showing an increase in cytotoxicity of up to 96-fold following irradiation. These findings establish that metallophilic interactions are a robust and effective tool for generating stable, self-delivering nanotherapeutics with exceptional targeting precision in vivo.
humanized_abstract:
Self-Assembling Molecular Drugs into Supramolecular Nanostructures In situ within Animals Self-assembling molecular drugs into supramolecular nanostructures address the simplicity of small-molecule chemotherapy as well as the need for effective tumor-targeting that is present in nanoparticle conjugates. This requires supramolecular interactions that are stable under the biological conditions found in an animal. Here, we utilize metallophilic interactions present between two cyclometalated palladium complexes to form stable supramolecular nanostructures in vivo. In a skin melanoma mouse model, these nanostructures provided a circulating half-life over 12 h and achieved tumor accumulation rates of up to 10.2% injected dose per gram.

Upon activation with green light, these complexes can stimulate a type I high efficiency photodynamic response to kill cancer cells. Increased cytotoxicity (up to 96X) has been verified in vitro following photactivation. In this paper we illustrate that exploitation of metallophilic interactions creates a new paradigm for the preparation of highly stable self assembling pro drug nanotherapeutic assemblies possessing excellent bio-targeting capability in vivo.

This requires supramolecular interactions that are stable under the biological conditions found in an animal. Here, we utilize metallophilic interactions present between two cyclometalated palladium complexes to form stable supramolecular nanostructures in vivo. In a skin melanoma mouse model, these nanostructures provided a circulating half-life over 12 h and achieved tumor accumulation rates of up to 10.2% injected dose per gram.

Upon activation with green light, these complexes can stimulate a type I high efficiency photodynamic response to kill cancer cells. Increased cytotoxicity (up to 96X) has been verified in vitro following photactivation. In this paper we illustrate that exploitation of metallophilic interactions creates a new paradigm for the preparation of highly stable self assembling pro drug nanotherapeutic assemblies possessing excellent bio-targeting capability in vivo.
